_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Clark County, KY. Formed 1793 from Bourbon and Fayette counties — the year after Kentucky statehood. Records begin from formation and are generally well-preserved. No major courthouse fire.

previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced as the county developed. Records transferred intact.
Replaced by the current 1940 building. No significant fire.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or disaster
record_losses.description: Clark County has experienced no major courthouse fire or disaster. Records run continuously from the 1793 formation. Some early loose papers show wear but bound volumes are intact. Clark County's record preservation is good.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} was in Clark County after 1793, the records almost certainly survive. Gaps reflect the ancestor's circumstances, not courthouse losses.
record_losses.source: Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives. Guide to Kentucky County Court Records.

wills_estates.completeness: Good — will books and estate inventories from formation.

court_minutes.completeness: Good — County Court order books from formation.

vital_records.repository: Kentucky Cabinet for Health and Family Services; KDLA

substitute_sources.description: Clark County was formed from Bourbon and Fayette counties. Records for Clark County families before 1793 are filed under the parent counties.
substitute_sources.repository: Fayette County Clerk; Bourbon County Clerk; KDLA
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for connecting families across the parent-county formation boundary. A family in 'Clark County' before 1793 was technically in Fayette or Bourbon.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1800-1940. Clark County first enumerated in 1800.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families across decades.

substitute_sources.source_type: Fort Boonesborough and early station records
substitute_sources.description: Records from Fort Boonesborough and other early stations document the first settlers in what became Clark County. These include petitions, station muster rolls, and land claims.
substitute_sources.repository: Filson Historical Society, Louisville; KDLA; Kentucky Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Links the earliest Bluegrass settlers to their Virginia origins and Revolutionary War service.
substitute_sources.source: Aron, How the West Was Lost (1996).

narrative_hooks: Clark County's records run continuously from 1793 — if {ancestor_name} was here, the courthouse has the paper trail. No fire, no flood, no war damage.
Before 1793, {ancestor_name}'s Clark County land was technically Fayette or Bourbon County — the parent-county records at KDLA are the key to the pre-formation period.
Fort Boonesborough station records may document {ancestor_name}'s earliest days in the Bluegrass — the station muster rolls and petitions are at the Filson Historical Society in Louisville.
Kentucky vital registration began in 1911 — the absence of a birth certificate for {ancestor_name} before that date is standard, not suspicious.
source_references: Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives. Guide to Kentucky County Court Records.
Aron, Stephen. How the West Was Lost. Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 1996.
Clark, Thomas D. A History of Kentucky. Lexington: John Bradford Press, 1960.
